perturbation.original_text: (d) As used in this Agreement, the term "Post-Term Period" shall mean a continuous uninterrupted period of two (2) years from the date of: (a) a transfer permitted under Section 14 of the Franchise Agreement; (b) expiration or termination of the Franchise Agreement (regardless of the cause for termination); (c) termination of Member's employment with Franchisee; and/or (d) a final order of a duly authorized arbitrator, panel of arbitrators, or a court of competent jurisdiction (after all appeals have been taken) with respect to any of the foregoing or with respect to the enforcement of this Agreement; either directly or indirectly (through, on behalf of, or in conjunction with any persons, partnership, corporation or entity).
perturbation.changed_text: (d) As used in this Agreement, the term "Post-Term Period" shall mean a continuous uninterrupted period of two (2) years from the date of: (a) a transfer permitted under Section 14 of the Franchise Agreement; (b) expiration or termination of the Franchise Agreement only if terminated by the Franchisee; (c) termination of Member's employment with Franchisee; and/or (d) a final order of a duly authorized arbitrator, panel of arbitrators, or a court of competent jurisdiction (after all appeals have been taken) with respect to any of the foregoing or with respect to the enforcement of this Agreement; either directly or indirectly (through, on behalf of, or in conjunction with any persons, partnership, corporation or entity).
perturbation.explanation: The modified text limits the definition of 'Post-Term Period' to only include termination of the Franchise Agreement by the Franchisee. This could conflict with state laws regarding termination rights and enforceability of non-compete clauses, depending on the specific circumstances of the termination. If the Franchisor terminates the agreement due to Franchisee breach, this revised definition could render the non-compete unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally invalidates non-compete agreements. While there are exceptions, including the sale of a business, the modified text attempts to limit the 'Post-Term Period' based on who terminates the franchise agreement. This limitation might be viewed as an unlawful restraint of trade, particularly if the franchisor terminates the agreement for reasons that are not directly tied to the employee's or franchisee's actions and then seeks to enforce the non-compete. The altered definition attempts to indirectly broaden the scope of when a non-compete is enforceable, potentially violating the spirit of Section 16600, which favors open competition.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by limiting the 'Post-Term Period' to only include termination of the Franchise Agreement by the Franchisee. This conflicts with California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which generally invalidates non-compete agreements. The snippet of BPC 16600 states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void". By limiting the 'Post-Term Period' based on who terminates the agreement, the modified text attempts to broaden the scope of when a non-compete is enforceable, potentially violating the spirit of Section 16600.

perturbation.original_text: Member shall not, during the term of the Franchise Agreement or thereafter, communicate, divulge or use, for any purpose other than the operation of the Franchised Business, any confidential information, knowledge, trade secrets or know-how which may be communicated to Member or which Member may learn by virtue of Member's relationship with Franchisee. All information, knowledge and know-how relating to Franchisor, its business plans, Franchised Businesses, or the System ("Confidential Information") is deemed confidential, except for information that Member can demonstrate came to Member's attention by lawful means prior to disclosure to Member; or which, at the time of the disclosure to Member, had become a part of the public domain.
perturbation.changed_text: Member shall not, during the term of the Franchise Agreement or thereafter, communicate, divulge or use, for any purpose other than the operation of the Franchised Business, any confidential information, knowledge, trade secrets or know-how which may be communicated to Member or which Member may learn by virtue of Member's relationship with Franchisee. All information, knowledge and know-how relating to Franchisor, its business plans, Franchised Businesses, or the System ("Confidential Information") is deemed confidential, including information already in the public domain.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that Confidential Information includes 'information already in the public domain', this directly contradicts the generally accepted legal definition of confidential information and trade secrets. Information readily available to the public cannot be legally protected as confidential. This creates an unenforceable clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA, adopted by many states, defines a trade secret as information that 'derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use.' Including information already in the public domain within the definition of Confidential Information directly contradicts this core requirement, rendering that portion of the agreement likely unenforceable under trade secret law.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly includes "information already in the public domain" within the definition of "Confidential Information." The Law Explanation states that the UTSA defines a trade secret as information that 'derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known'. Including public information directly contradicts this core requirement. The Perturbation Explanation also clarifies this contradiction, stating that information readily available to the public cannot be legally protected as confidential. This directly clashes with the standard legal understanding and definition of confidential information, making the clause likely unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Member covenants and agrees that during the Post-Term Period (defined below), except as otherwise approved in writing by Franchisor, Member shall not, either directly or indirectly, own, manage, engage in, be employed by, advise, make loans to, consult for, or have any other interest in any Competitive Business that is, or intends to operate, within a three (3) mile radius of the premises of your Franchised Business or within a three (3) mile radius of any Franchised Business then-operating or under construction to operate under the System.
perturbation.changed_text: Member covenants and agrees that during the Post-Term Period (defined below), except as otherwise approved in writing by Franchisor, Member shall not, either directly or indirectly, own, manage, engage in, be employed by, advise, make loans to, consult for, or have any other interest in any business that is, or intends to operate, that sells food within the United States of America.
perturbation.explanation: The change broadens the scope of the non-compete from a 3-mile radius around a Pizza Fusion franchise to any food-selling business within the entire United States. This is almost certainly an unreasonable and unenforceable geographic restriction.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 188 states that a non-compete is unreasonable if 'the restraint is greater than is needed to protect the promisee's legitimate interest' or 'the promisee's need is outweighed by the hardship to the promisor and the likely injury to the public.' A nationwide ban on working for any food-selling business after leaving a Pizza Fusion franchise is far broader than necessary to protect the franchisor's interests and imposes undue hardship on the former employee. Such a broad restriction is highly likely to be deemed unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the non-compete scope to any food-selling business in the U.S., whereas the original limited it to a 3-mile radius. The Law Explanation states this is likely unenforceable as it is 'greater than is needed to protect the promisee's legitimate interest' based on Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 188. This suggests the modified contract directly contradicts established principles regarding reasonable non-compete agreements, making it excessively broad and therefore likely invalid. The scraped snippets are not applicable.
